problem_summary: 2010 MCM Problem B (discrete), "Geographic Profiling / Predicting a Serial Criminal's Next Crime Location." The task: given the spatiotemporal data (locations + times) of a serial criminal's prior crimes, build a method that prioritizes a search area so the next crime is likely to fall in the highest-priority region. This team (Control 7947) reframed the classic geographic-profiling problem — which traditionally predicts the criminal's *residence* — into predicting the *next crime location*, built a family of competing spatial / spatiotemporal / aggregate probability models over a discretized grid, defined a self-consistent performance metric (hit score) and a per-criminal accuracy score, validated against 124 real crimes from 9 serial offenders, and concluded honestly that all non-random methods perform about equally (next crime lands in ~top 10% of the search area).
models_used: Grid discretization of the search area: square region centered at the centroid of past crimes, side = 2x max pairwise crime distance, partitioned into sectors S_{i,j} of size Δs; probability assumed constant within a sector P(y)=P(S_{i,j})

Spatial centralization models — P(S_{i,j}) = 1 / d(S_{i,j}, C) with the focal point C chosen four ways: (1) Centroid (arithmetic mean of crime coords), (2) Harmonic mean, (3) Circle Center (midpoint of the two farthest-apart crimes), (4) Median (median of longitudes and latitudes, robust to outlier crimes)
Probability distance (distance-decay) models — P(S_{i,j}) = Σ_k f(d(S_{i,j}, x_k)) summed over all prior crimes, with two decay kernels: Linear f(d)=α−βd and Exponential f(d)=e^{−γd}

Standard-deviation geometric models (baseline/naive): standard-deviation rectangles and trend-oriented standard-deviation ellipses (axes 2cσ_lon, 2cσ_lat; c=1→68th pct, c=2→95th pct) — included as rudimentary benchmarks, acknowledged as not directly comparable since they give no intra-region probability ordering

Spatiotemporal extensions — per-crime temporal weight w_i = (t_i − t_1)/t_n + k with offset k=0.1 (so newest crime weighted ~10x the oldest); yields Temporal Centroid, Temporal Harmonic Mean, and Temporal distance-decay P(S_{i,j}) = Σ_k w_k f(d(S_{i,j}, x_k)). Temporal Exponential Decay was the single best individual model

Aggregation (expert-pooling) models — Linear Opinion Pool P = Σ_k W_k P_k (weighted sum of model probability surfaces) and Logarithmic Opinion Pool P = Π_k P_k^{W_k} (weighted geometric mean); framed as the classic axiomatic-vs-Bayesian expert-aggregation problem, axiomatic chosen per Clemen & Winkler
Random/uniform model as the null baseline (expected hit score 0.5)
key_techniques: Custom evaluation metric — the 'hit score' H = |B|/|S|, where B = {sectors with predicted probability > that of the sector actually containing the next crime}; i.e., the fraction of prioritized area searched before finding the true next crime. Lower is better; directly tied to law-enforcement utility (find an area, not a point)

Walk-forward / leave-future-out backtesting for a per-criminal 'accuracy score' Z = (1/(n−3)) Σ_{k=4}^{n} H_k: sequentially reveal crimes, predict the next, average hit scores. Starting at the 4th crime is justified by the criminological definition of 'serial' (≥3 crimes)

Explicit search-area definition as a modeling decision — recognized that the hit score is gameable by inflating the search area (peripheral sectors dilute it), and pinned it down (2x max pairwise distance square) — a subtlety the team notes the published literature leaves unspecified

Insight that decay-constant magnitudes (α, β, γ) are irrelevant for a *single* model because the hit score depends only on the *relative ranking* of sectors, but *do* matter once models are aggregated (sums/products mix magnitudes) — they then set γ = sqrt(2/δ̄) and β via α−βδ̄=0 using the mean pairwise distance δ̄ to put models on a comparable scale

2^k factorial experimental design to search the aggregation space — each candidate model is in/out of the equally-weighted pool; mean hit score computed over all combinations to find the best ensemble (temporal exponential decay + circle center)
Statistical honesty via 95% confidence intervals on every model's hit score, used to show the differences between methods are statistically insignificant — overlapping CIs, not just point estimates
Real-data validation: hand-compiled 124-crime, 9-offender dataset (Beltway Snipers, Sutcliffe, Dale Hausner, plus robberies/assaults) cross-verified against police reports, news, and SpotCrime.com
Distance-metric abstraction — keep d() generic (Euclidean / Manhattan / travel-time) and justify the Euclidean choice rather than assume it
why_it_won: "Judges reward a team that turns an open, ill-posed prompt into a crisp, testable scientific question and then answers it with intellectual honesty — this paper does exactly that. (1) Problem reframing: it consciously pivots geographic profiling from 'find the residence' (what the literature does) to 'predict the next crime location' (what the prompt actually wants) and argues why the two are related yet distinct — showing genuine command of the domain. (2) Falsifiable comparison: instead of championing one model, it builds ~11 competing models on common footing, invents a utility-aligned metric (hit score) and a backtesting accuracy score, and lets the data decide. (3) Honest negative result: it reports that no method beats any other at statistical significance and that some criminals (the Columbus rapist, Z=0.533) are essentially unpredictable — and corroborates this with the literature (Snook et al.: complex profiling is no better than a minimally-trained human). Surfacing a null result, with CIs, is far more credible than an over-claimed winner. (4) Self-aware metric engineering: catching that the hit score is inflatable by enlarging the search area, and that decay constants matter only after aggregation, are exactly the second-order insights that separate O from Meritorious. (5) Real validation on 124 verified crimes plus a vivid Peter Sutcliffe case study (10.6% hit score isolating 2,941 of 27,818 km²) makes it concrete and memorable. (6) An exemplary Executive Summary that hands law enforcement a usability checklist and frank caveats — the deliverable feels operational, not academic."
section_highlights: "Structure mirrors the scientific method and is unusually disciplined for a 4-day paper. Two-tier front matter: a one-paragraph Summary plus a full Executive Summary written *for the end user* (law enforcement) — it states when the models apply (evidence of serial nature, accuracy score, available resources) and three blunt limitations (no geographic features, no offender demographics, may not beat a trained human), ending with the recommendation that the tool *complement* not replace investigators. Section 3 ('Constructing a Model') cleanly factors out everything common to all methods — notation (x_i, t_i, P(y)), discretization, the hit-score metric, the accuracy score, and the search-area definition — *before* introducing any specific model, so Sections 4–5 read as plug-ins into a shared framework. Models are presented as a deliberate progression: naive (std-dev rectangles/ellipses) → spatial (centralization, distance-decay) → spatiotemporal (time-weighted) → aggregate (opinion pools), each motivated by a weakness in the prior tier. Results are visual and comparative: a bar chart of all model hit scores, a table with 95% CIs, a hit-score-vs-crimes-observed line chart, and an aggregation-weight sweep (Figure 6) showing the search surface morph as circle-center/exponential weights shift. The Peter Sutcliffe case study grounds abstractions in a real map with a concrete km² payoff. A literature survey with ~20 real references (Rossmo, O'Leary, Snook, Brown) frames the work and is used substantively in the conclusions, not just cited decoratively. Future Work is specific and credible: standardized datasets, feature-space modeling, offender demographics, cost-benefit of commercial profiling software."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Tiny, self-compiled dataset — only 9 criminals / 124 crimes, no governmental data; the team admits no standardized serial-crime corpus exists, so absolute hit-score numbers and the 'top 10%' headline are weakly powered and not comparable to prior literature

The headline finding is essentially a null result: all non-random models are statistically indistinguishable, so the elaborate model zoo, temporal weighting, and aggregation machinery yield no measurable improvement over the simplest centroid model — impressive rigor, limited payoff

Harmonic-mean focal point formula C = n / Σ(1/x_i) is applied to 2D coordinate vectors x_i, which is mathematically ill-defined / dimensionally suspect for points in R^2 (component-wise reciprocal of longitude/latitude has no clear geographic meaning, and is sensitive to the coordinate origin) — a likely sloppy spot

Models ignore all real geography: no roads, rivers, coastlines, population density, or target-type (e.g., banks); probability mass can fall in implausible locations (water, uninhabited areas). The team flags this but doesn't mitigate it

The hit score rewards only relative ranking, so single-model decay-constant choices are untested/arbitrary; and the metric is admittedly sensitive to the (somewhat arbitrary) 2x-max-pairwise-distance search-area definition — different choices would change the reported percentages

Temporal weighting parameters (k=0.1, '10x' newest-vs-oldest) are chosen by intuition, not fit or sensitivity-tested; despite the construction, temporal models barely differ from their spatial counterparts (0.092 vs 0.094)

Stationarity / single-offender assumption: breaks under copy-cats, linkage errors, or a criminal who relocates; the case study even predicts the next Sutcliffe crime around Bradford without out-of-sample confirmation since he was arrested

Some figure data in the markdown render is garbled (the Sutcliffe heatmap table and Figure 4 bar labels are corrupted/duplicated), though this is an OCR/conversion artifact rather than an original-paper flaw
reusable_patterns: Design a custom, utility-aligned evaluation metric early, then let it drive everything. The hit score (fraction of prioritized area searched to find the target) is the spine of the whole paper — define what 'good' means in the stakeholder's terms before building models. Reusable for any ranking/prioritization problem (search, triage, recommendation).

Walk-forward backtesting on real historical data to produce a *per-instance* trust score (accuracy score Z), and report it per case — this directly answers 'how much should the user trust this on THIS input?', a question judges love.

Build a *family* of competing models on a shared scaffold and compare them head-to-head with confidence intervals, rather than advocating one model. Factor all shared machinery (notation, discretization, metric) into a common section so each model is a small, comparable plug-in.
Report and embrace negative / null results with statistical backing (overlapping 95% CIs) and corroborate them against the literature — honesty reads as credibility, not weakness.

Use expert-aggregation theory (linear and logarithmic opinion pools) as a principled, citable way to ensemble heterogeneous sub-models; combine with a 2^k factorial design to systematically search which sub-models to include.
Discretize a continuous domain into a grid and assign each cell a probability — a clean, general template for turning 'predict a location' into a tractable, rankable scoring problem.

Spot scale-invariance subtleties: identify which parameters are irrelevant under your metric (decay constants for a single ranked model) versus which become relevant after a transformation (aggregation), and normalize via a problem-derived scale (mean pairwise distance δ̄).

Add a temporal-weighting layer w_i = (t_i−t_1)/t_n + k to up-weight recent observations when a process drifts over time — a lightweight way to extend any spatial/static model into a spatiotemporal one.

Write the Executive Summary as an operational decision aid for the named stakeholder: an applicability checklist + explicit limitations + a 'use as a complement, not a replacement' recommendation. Anchor abstractions with one concrete, real-world case study (Sutcliffe: 2,941 of 27,818 km²) for memorability.
